coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots fluorescence quenching assay for detection of mercury (II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chaghaghazardi, Mosayeb
Kashanian, Soheila
Nazari, Maryam
Omidfar, Kobra
Joseph, Yvonne
Rahimi, Parvaneh
coredata.dc:description: Mercury is a highly toxic and potentially bioaccumulative heavy metal ion that can cause severe health problems in humans even at very low concentrations. Thus, the development of a simple, rapid, and sensitive assay for the effective detection of mercury ions at trace levels is of great importance. Here, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQD) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal treatment of chitosan in the presence of thiourea and citric acid with a quantum yield (QY) up to 33.0 % and used as a selective fluorescent probe to detect mercury ions (Hg2+). The effect of pH, ionic strength, and time on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQD were investigated and optimized. The synthesized N,S-CQD showed ultrasensitive ability to detect Hg2+ ions in the water samples, also in the presence of 11 interfering metal ions, with a low detection limit (∼4 nM) over a wide linear range from ∼5–160 nM. The sensing performance of N,S-CQD probe in real sample applications was evaluated by the detection of Hg2+ in lake water samples, which confirmed its potential application in environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Mercury sensor
Fluorescence quenching
Chitosan
Nitrogen and sulphur doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N,S-CQDs Synthesis and quantum yield determination
Investigating the effect of pH, ionic strength (NaCl) and time on fluorescence intensity of N,S-CODs
Hg 2+ sensing via fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs
Detection of Hg2+ in real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Biosensor Research Center of Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Institute of Tehran University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We, the authors, would like to thank the Biosensor Research Center of Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Institute of Tehran University of Medical Sciences for their financial support (Grant ID: 1128).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the synthesized N,S-CQDs. (b) Elemental analysis of N,S-CQDs obtained from EDX analysis. (c) FTIR spectra of the Chitosan, Thiourea, Citric acid and the N,S-CQDs obtained from hydrothermal synthesis.

(a) Absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs solution; Inset: The N,S-CQDs solution under white light (light brown) and UV (green) irradiation. (b) Excitation (blue) and fluorescence emission (red) spectra of N,S-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Effect of pH, N,S-CQD concentration of 50 ppm, and 30 °C temperature in double distilled water. (b) Effect of ionic strength (NaCl), N,S-CQD concentration of 50 ppm, pH 7.0, and 30 °C temperature. (c) Effect of time, pH 7.0, N,S-CQD concentration of 50 ppm, 30 °C temperature, and double distilled water. Spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 330 nm and maximum emission wavelength of 420 nm.
(a) The fluorescence response of the N,S-CQD upon addition of different concentration of Hg(II) (b) Calibration curve of Hg(II) sensor under the optimal conditions (pH 7.0, record time = 5 min).
Selectivity analysis of N,S-CQDs fluorescence quenching towards mercury ion concentration of 0.1 µM and other various metal ions in concentration of 1.0 μM.
Elemental analysis of N,S-CQDs obtained from EDX analysis.
Figures of merit for some of the formerly reported CQD-based Hg(II) fluorescence sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results in real samples for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots fluorescence quenching assay for detection of mercury (II)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury is a highly toxic and potentially bioaccumulative heavy metal ion that can cause severe health problems in humans even at very low concentrations. Thus, the development of a simple, rapid, and sensitive assay for the effective detection of mercury ions at trace levels is of great importance. Here, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQD) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal treatment of chitosan in the presence of thiourea and citric acid with a quantum yield (QY) up to 33.0 % and used as a selective fluorescent probe to detect mercury ions (Hg2+). The effect of pH, ionic strength, and time on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQD were investigated and optimized. The synthesized N,S-CQD showed ultrasensitive ability to detect Hg2+ ions in the water samples, also in the presence of 11 interfering metal ions, with a low detection limit (∼4 nM) over a wide linear range from ∼5–160 nM. The sensing performance of N,S-CQD probe in real sample applications was evaluated by the detection of Hg2+ in lake water samples, which confirmed its potential application in environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution caused by heavy metal ions is a serious global problem because they can bioaccumulate in the environment and endanger human health [1]. Amino acid as an important building block of proteins and enzymes form potent mercury- complexes that can cause severe hepatotoxicity and neurotoxicity. Thus, Hg2+ is regarded as one of the most harmful heavy metal ions [2]. It principally made by volcanic emissions and diverse industrial anthropogenic activities in electronic manufacturing, waste incineration, battery production, mining operations, and coal combustion, censoriously threatening aquatic ecology and human health. Since the mechanism of the human body is such that it is not able to remove mercury, mercury gradually accumulates in the body and its concentration increases with the passage of time. In case of exposure to even low amounts of mercury, this heavy metal as a non-removable pollutant in the human body causes serious damage to the brain and nervous system, kidney, liver, digestive system, lungs and even the immune system [3]. The maximum permissible level of Hg2+ in drinking water is 6.0 μg/L based on the World Health Organization (WHO), whereas the recommended level is 2.0 μg/L according to the United States Environment Protection Agency (EPA) [4]. Due to the high environmental toxicity and harmful effects on humans, the determination of Hg2+ and regular monitoring of water sources and environmental protection have become an extremely important issue [5]. A variety of analytical methods have been employed to quantify Hg2+, including electrochemical methods [6], atomic spectroscopy [7], chromatography [8], mass spectrometry [9], or a combination of them [10].

The fluorescence-based approach using several quantum dots (QDs) (like CdS, CdTe, CdSe and their compositions) has gained considerable interest in the detection of Hg2+ and other heavy metals due to advantages such as superior optical and chemical stability and rapid response [11–13]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and selectivity toward a quencher species are a criterion used to determine the fluorescence technique sensitivity. Greater selectivity toward a quencher and higher fluorophore QY make a method more sensitive and cause a lower limit of detection [14]. To improve the fluorescence QY and sensitivity, QDs with tunable size, higher QY, and excellent optical properties were developed [15]. However, the complicated synthesis methods and potential toxicity of these fluorescent probes limit their further application. [16]. Unlike other QDs, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered as a great alternative to the conventional heavy metal QDs due to their ease of synthesis and modification, lower toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, superior photostability, and low cost [17,18]. Thus, CQDs have been employed in various application areas, including bioimaging [19], photocatalysis [20], fluorescent ink [21], lasers [22], light-emitting diode [23], energy conversion/storage devices [24], fluorescent probe [25], and optical sensors [26]. Despite their outstanding advantages, the QY of CQDs is low and requires improvement. The incorporation of heteroatoms into CQDs create n- and p-type carriers and lead to a significant change in their energy states and electronic structures, improving the optical properties and QY of CQDs [27–29]. Among the various heteroatoms, nitrogen with n-type doping properties is the most commonly used dopant to improve the optical efficiency of QDs [30,31]. In addition to nitrogen, sulphur was also used as a dopant, which not only improves the fluorescence intensity but also causes a red shift in the absorption spectra [32]. It was recently reported that co-doping with N and S elements is an effective strategy to promote the photosenitivity of CQDs. In this regard, Shen et al. [33] prepared a new fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) using the graphene oxide-assisted synthesis technique. The prepared N,S-CQDs showed a wide linear response to a range of water-soluble metal ions via fluorescence quenching method. In another study, using the pyrolysis carbonization method, Ye et al. [34] developed four different N,S-CQDs with various QYs from pigeon eggs, manure, and feathers via a simple one-pot synthesis method. The three prepared probes were highly selective and sensitive to Hg2+/Fe3+ ions with low limit of detection values of 10.3 and 60.9 nM.

Using hydrothermal treatment of chitosan in the presence of thiourea and citric acid at 200 °C, we have developed a simple and cheap method to synthesize N,S-CQDs with green emission and a high QY. Compared with the previous studies, the developed method in this study does not require expensive reagents, strong acid, base, organic solvent, and further surface passivation, which are the specific features of the developed technique compare to other reported techniques [4,16,34–36]. In addition, the reaction time is shorter than the reported approaches (<4h) [14,37].

Chitosan (low molecular weight), citric acid, and thiourea were bought from Merck company and were used as obtained. HgCl2 was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Hg2+ stock solution was prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of its chloride salts in double distilled water (DDW). Other metal chloride salts as interferences also were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and their ions solutions were prepared by dissolving them in DDW adjusting the pH solution at 7.0 and fixing the temperature at 30 °C. The concentration of interfering ions was 1 µM and Hg2+ solution was 0.1 µM.

Disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from Merck company and used to prepare phosphate buffer solutions (PB). The real sample was obtained from Taqe Bostan Lake of Kermanshah, Iran. The sample was filtered using a 0.45 µm paper filter.

A Teflon lined autoclave was used to hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-CQDs. A VARIAN (model: Carry Eclips, Australia) fluorescence spectrometer (Australia) was used to record the fluorescence spectra. Further, a VARIAN spectrophotometer (Cary 100 Bio, Australia) was used to record UV–Vis absorption spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, model Zeiss-EM10C Company) was used to characterize the morphological properties of the sample and observe the sample at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. A Misonix sonicator (Misonix- S3000, USA) was sued to sonicate the dilute aqueous solution of the sample. One drop of the sample was put on a Formvar carbon film on a copper grid 300 mesh (EMS-USA) and was dried completely at room temperature.

Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker FTIR spectrophotometer (model Alpha, USA). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Rontec Germany) was used to analyze the elements of N,S-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N,S-CQDs Synthesis and quantum yield determination
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-CQDs were synthesized as follows: 0.50 g of chitosan, 0.10 g of citric acid, and 0.50 g of thiourea were dissolved in DDW and placed at room temperature overnight under mild stirring. The solution was delivered to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting brown colored solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 45 min. For elimination of unreacted material and more purification, the solution was then purified using a 12 kD membrane dialysis in double distilled water for 48 h. The resulting sample was dried at 60 °C, and the obtained black-brown powder was denoted as N,S-CQDs. QY of synthesized N,S-CQDs was calculated by the relative comparison procedure, using freshly prepared anthracene in ethanol as a standard for relative QY. To calculate the QY, five concentrations of standard and N,S-CQDs in ethanol were used, and all had absorbance less than 0.1 at 330 nm. The QY of anthracene in ethanol is 0.27. The N,S-CQDs samples were dissolved in ethanol.

For calculation of N,S-CQDs QY at a 365 nm excitation wavelength the following equation (1) was used [29]:
(1)
ϕ
N . S - C Q D
=
ϕ R
I
N . S - C Q D
I R
A R
A
N . S - C Q D
η
N . S - C Q D
2
η R
2

The “ ϕ ”, “A”, “η”, and “I” are referred to as the QY, absorbance, refractive index of the solvent, and measured integrated emission intensity, respectively. The subscript “R” demonstrates a standard substance.

A stock solution of synthesized N,S-CQD (1000 ppm) was prepared in DDW and utilized during the experiments. 100 µL of N,S-CQDs and various amounts of the 100 ppm Hg2+ solution were added to 2 mL of PB (0.01 M, pH 7) and the final volume was adjusted to 4.0 mL by adding DDW to obtain a final concentration of 25 ppm for N,S-CQDs. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after 5 min at 30 °C. The excitation wavelength tuned at 330 nm and the decreased intensity of the fluorescence emission at 420 nm, in comparison with that of the blank solution, was considered as the analytical signal.

The real sample was prepared from Taqe Bostan Lake of Kermanshah, Iran. The sample was filtered with a 0.45 µm paper filter. The different volumes of a 10-6 M Hg2+ solution were spiked in the solution containing 1.0 mL of the phosphate buffer solution (0.01 M, pH 7.0) and 100 μL of the N,S-CQD solution (1000 ppm). The final volume was adjusted to 4.0 mL with filtered Lake water to achieve final concentrations of 20, 40, and 60 nM of Hg2+. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after 5 min, and the reduced intensity of the fluorescence emission at 420 nm, versus that of the blank solution, was regarded as the analytical signal.

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of the synthesized N,S-CQDs. A spherical morphology and an average particle size of 30 nm can be observed for the prepared nanoparticles. EDX analysis of the synthesized N,S-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1b and Table 1 , verifying the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulphur elements in the structure of N,S-CQDs.

Fig. 1c indicates the FTIR spectra of chitosan, thiourea, citric acid, and N,S-CQDs. Comparing the spectra of chitosan, thiourea, and citric acid as precursors and spectra of N,S-CQDs, the changes of stretching bands of related functional groups from precursors to N,S-CQDs as product confirmed the presence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, and sulfur functional groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs. The absorption peak at 1150 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O groups. Furthermore, the peak at 1394 cm−1 was ascribed to the CC stretching vibration. The peak at 1550 was related to the NH bending vibration of amine group. The shoulder peak at 1715 cm−1 was related to the CO stretching vibration, which confirmed the presence of the carbonyl group. The absorption peak at 1617 cm−1 was ascribed to the aromatic CC in the structure of N,S-CQDs. The weak absorption peak at 2930 cm−1 was related to the weak vibration of SH [38]. The peak at 3235 cm−1 was ascribed to the NH stretching vibration. TEM, FTIR and EDX analysis confirm that N,S-CQDs have been successfully synthesized.

UV–Vis spectrum of the N,S-CQDs (Fig. 2 a) illustrates three major absorption bands at 220, 260, and 340 nm, corresponding to carbon-carbon double bond (π → π*), carbonyl double bond n → π*, and aromatic ring π → π* transitions, respectively [39,40]. The N,S-CQDs solution in water had a light brown color under normal light conditions, while it emitted green light under UV irradiation (Fig. 2a inset).
Fig. 2b displays the excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs. As indicated, the maximum emission was at 420 nm, while excitation happened at 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the effect of pH, ionic strength (NaCl) and time on fluorescence intensity of N,S-CODs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of solutions can influence the function of sensors. The surface of N,S-CQDs is full of amine, hydroxyl, sulfide, and carboxylic acid functional groups. Hence, a changed pH value can cause a change in the charge of these groups due to the protonation and deprotonation, thereby influencing their fluorescence intensity [14,41,42]. Fig. 3 a indicates the impact of pH on the emission of 50 ppm N,S-CQDs sample at 30 °C. As seen, in acidic or basic pH, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs decreased, and the maximum fluorescence intensity happened at pH 7.0. Hence, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal pH value to increase the sensor sensitivity.

Ionic strength is another critical factor that affect the fluorescent emission of fluorophores. To evaluate the ionic strength effect on the fluorescence intensity of prepared N,S-CQDs, the fluorescence emission of 50 ppm N,S-CQDs solutions containing different concentrations of NaCl salt were recorded at pH 7.0 and 30 °C. As can be seen (Fig. 3b), the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing NaCl concentration and ionic strength of the medium. The highest emission was recorded at zero ionic strength (double distilled water).

Fig. 3c shows the impact of time on the fluorescence emission of N,S-CQDs. The fluorescence emission of a 50 ppm CQD at 30 °C and pH 7 was recorded each 5 min at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm and emission wavelength of 420 nm. The results showed that N,S-CQDs had excellent photostability with time and no bleaching or quenching occurs within an hour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg 2+ sensing via fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing performance of N,S-CQDs towards Hg2+ under optimal conditions (pH 7.0 and 5 min incubation time) was investigated. As expected, with increasing the concentration of Hg2+ the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs decreased gradually. Fig. 4 displays the linear relationship between Hg2+ concentration and fluorescence quenching of N,S CODs (I0-I) in a linear range of 5.2–159.8 nM with the sensitivity of 0.9927 nM−1 where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of the N,S-CQD solution before and after addition of Hg2+, respectively. Given the linear equation shown in Fig. 4, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of this method were calculates as 4.0 and 13.4 nM, respectively. To calculate LOD and LOQ the formulas of 3.3(standard deviation (SD)/slope) and 10(SD/slope) were used, respectively. Table 2 shows the comparison of performance characteristics of the prepared sensor based on N,S-CQDs with several previously reported doped CQD based on different elements (N, S, P, and B) for Hg2+ fluorescence sensing. As shown, the N,S-CQDs in this work could be easily synthesized without any post-synthesis modifications. In addition, LOD of the proposed sensor is lower than most of the reported studies as indicated in the Table 2, suggesting the high sensitivity of the N,S-CQDs in Hg2+ detection.

Besides the sensitivity, selectivity is another important parameter of a sensing method. Therefore, fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs was investigated in the presence of 11 other metal ions with the concentration of 0.1 μM. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , an obvious fluorescence quenching can be seen with addition of Hg2+ to N,S-CQDs solution, while the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs by other metal ions was approximately ignorable. These results verify the selectivity of the prepared N,S-CQDs toward Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To further display the applicability of the synthesized N,S-CQDs, they were used to determine Hg2+ in Taqe Bostan Lake water as real sample while pH is adjusted at 7.0 using 0.01 M PB. As indicated in Table 3 , the achieved recoveries were in the range of 94–106%, which proves that the proposed sensor is highly promising for the determination of Hg2+ in practical applications.

Green emitting N,S-CQDs with a high quantum yield (33.0%) and excellent fluorescence stability were synthesized using a facile thermal method from chitosan, thiourea, and citric acid without any more modification/passivation. The prepared N,S-CQDs were used to detect Hg2+ in water in the presence of interfering metal ions and provided desirable selectivity and sensitivity over a wide linear range from 5.2 to 159.8 nM with a LOD of 4.0 nM. This fluorescent probe was successfully utilized for the detection of Hg2+ in river water samples with good recoveries. Based on the obtained results, N,S-CQDs can be used as a selective fluorescence probe for the quantification of Hg2+ in environmental water samples.

Mosayeb Chaghaghazardi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Soheila Kashanian: Supervision. Maryam Nazari: Data curation, Visualization. Kobra Omidfar: Methodology, Validation. Yvonne Joseph: Writing – review & editing. Parvaneh Rahimi: Software, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We, the authors, would like to thank the Biosensor Research Center of Endocrinology and Metabolism Research Institute of Tehran University of Medical Sciences for their financial support (Grant ID: 1128).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots as a “Turn-Off” Fluorescent Probes for Highly Selective and Sensitive Detection of Mercury (II) Ions
